subject: ap-chemistry
title: Intermolecular Forces & Enthalpy of Combustion
stimulus:
A student places a mixture of plastic beads consisting of polypropylene (PP) and polyvinyl chloride (PVC) in a
1.0 L beaker containing distilled water. After stirring the contents of the beaker vigorously, the student observes
that the beads of one type of plastic sink to the bottom of the beaker and the beads of the other type of plastic
float on the water. The chemical structures of PP and PVC are represented by the diagrams below, which show
segments of each polymer.

parts.prompt:
Given that the spacing between polymer chains in PP and PVC is similar, the beads that sink are made of
which polymer? Explain.
PP is synthesized from propene, $C_3H_6$, and PVC is synthesized from vinyl chloride, $C_2H_3Cl$. The structures of
the molecules are shown below.
parts.scoring_notes: PVC sinks. With similar chain spacing (similar volume per repeating unit), the denser polymer sinks. PVC has Cl substituents (M = 35.5 g/mol) while PP has CH$_3$ substituents (M = 15 g/mol). The higher mass of Cl makes PVC denser than PP and denser than water.

scoring_points.description: For PVC with correct reasoning about Cl mass giving higher density: 1 point

required_elements: PVC has Cl substituents which are heavier than the CH$_3$ substituents on PP. With similar chain spacing, PVC has greater mass per unit volume (higher density) than PP, making it denser than water.
alternatives.correct_example: PVC beads sink. Both polymers have similar spacing between chains, so density differences arise from the mass of the substituent groups. PVC has Cl atoms (M = 35.5 g/mol) per repeating unit, whereas PP has CH$_3$ groups (M = 15 g/mol). The heavier Cl atoms give PVC a higher density than water, so it sinks.

wrong_examples: Saying PP sinks — PP is less dense than water because CH$_3$ groups are lighter than Cl atoms.
common_traps: Density = mass/volume. With similar volumes (similar chain spacing), the heavier monomer unit gives higher density.

parts.prompt:
The boiling point of liquid propene (226 K) is lower than the boiling point of liquid vinyl chloride (260 K).
Account for this difference in terms of the types and strengths of intermolecular forces present in each
liquid.
parts.scoring_notes: Both liquids experience London dispersion forces (LDFs). Vinyl chloride also has significant dipole–dipole forces because the C–Cl bond is very polar (Cl is much more electronegative than C), creating a permanent dipole. The additional dipole–dipole interactions in vinyl chloride make its IMFs stronger overall, requiring more energy to overcome them, hence a higher boiling point.

scoring_points.description: For correctly identifying dipole–dipole forces in vinyl chloride as the reason for its higher boiling point: 1 point

required_elements: Vinyl chloride has dipole–dipole interactions in addition to LDFs because it is polar (C–Cl bond creates permanent dipole).
Propene is essentially nonpolar; only LDFs.
Stronger IMFs in vinyl chloride → higher boiling point.
alternatives.correct_example: Both propene and vinyl chloride have London dispersion forces. Vinyl chloride also has dipole–dipole interactions because the C–Cl bond is highly polar (Cl is much more electronegative than C), creating a significant permanent molecular dipole. The additional dipole–dipole forces in vinyl chloride make its intermolecular forces stronger overall, requiring more thermal energy to separate the molecules; hence, vinyl chloride has a higher boiling point.

wrong_examples: Saying vinyl chloride has hydrogen bonding — there is no O–H or N–H bond in vinyl chloride.
Attributing the difference only to LDF strength — both molecules are similar in size; the key difference is the polarity of vinyl chloride.
common_traps: Vinyl chloride (C$_2$H$_3$Cl) has a net dipole from the C–Cl bond. Propene (C$_3$H$_6$) is essentially nonpolar. Extra dipole–dipole forces in vinyl chloride raise its boiling point.

parts.prompt:
Using the table of standard enthalpies of formation below, determine whether the combustion of 2.00 mol
of propene releases more, less, or the same amount of energy that 2.00 mol of vinyl chloride releases. Justify
your answer with a calculation. The balanced equation for the combustion of 2.00 mol of propene
is 2 $C_3H_6$(g) + 9 $O_2$(g) → 6 $CO_2$(g) + 6 $H_2O$(g).

| Substance | $C_2H_3Cl$(g) | $C_3H_6$(g) | $CO_2$(g) | $H_2O$(g) | HCl(g) | $O_2$(g) |
|-----------|--------------|------------|----------|----------|--------|--------|
| $\Delta H°_f$ (kJ/mol) | 37 | 21 | −394 | −242 | −92 | 0 |
parts.scoring_notes: For 2 mol propene: $\Delta H° = 6(-394) + 6(-242) - [2(21) + 9(0)] = -2364 - 1452 - 42 = -3858$ kJ. For 2 mol vinyl chloride combustion: $\Delta H° = 4(-394) + 2(-242) + 2(-92) - [2(37) + 5(0)] = -1576 - 484 - 184 - 74 = -2318$ kJ (given as −2300 kJ/rxn from stimulus). Propene combustion releases more energy (3858 kJ vs. 2300 kJ).

scoring_points.description: For 'more energy from propene' with a correct Hess's law calculation: 1 point

required_elements: Comparison to −2300 kJ for vinyl chloride confirms propene releases more energy.
alternatives.correct_example:
Propene combustion: $\Delta H° = \sum n\Delta H°_f(\text{products}) - \sum n\Delta H°_f(\text{reactants})$
$= [6(-394) + 6(-242)] - [2(21) + 9(0)]$
$= (-2364 - 1452) - 42 = -3858\text{ kJ}$
Vinyl chloride combustion releases −2300 kJ. Since $|-3858| > |-2300|$, combustion of 2.00 mol propene releases more energy.

wrong_examples: Using products minus reactants in the wrong order — must be $\sum\Delta H°_f(\text{products}) - \sum\Delta H°_f(\text{reactants})$.
Forgetting to multiply $\Delta H°_f$ values by their stoichiometric coefficients.
common_traps: Hess's law: $\Delta H°_{rxn} = \sum n_p \Delta H°_f(\text{products}) - \sum n_r \Delta H°_f(\text{reactants})$. Must multiply each $\Delta H°_f$ by its coefficient.
